body:
### Problem

Stopping a process spawned by `vim.system` using `:kill()` or `uv.kill` results in descendants of that process getting orphaned. Presumably, the process group is not getting sent the termination signal. The same does not occur when using `jobstart`/`jobstop`

### Steps to reproduce

Run the following:
```lua
nvim -l - <<EOF
local obj = vim.system({'bash', '-c', 'sleep 30 | sleep 30 | sleep 30'})
vim.cmd("sleep 1")
obj:kill()
vim.cmd("pgrep -f 'sleep 30'")
EOF
```

### Expected behavior

The descendants should also be terminated, as in `jobstart`/`jobstop`

### Nvim version (nvim -v)

NVIM v0.13.0-dev-386+g858cd3d106

### Vim (not Nvim) behaves the same?

no

### Operating system/version

Linux 6.19.9

### Terminal name/version

alacritty 0.16.1 (42f49eeb)

### $TERM environment variable

tmux-256color

### Installation

AppImage

comment_data.body:
This is, at this point, the 5th case where we are solving problems in `vim.system` that were already solved in `jobstart()` and the internal `proc` layer. In this case, `os_proc_tree_kill` exists to deal with this, but `vim.spawn` doesn't use it.

We really need to have 1 job-control layer, not 2 parallel ones. That likely means `vim.spawn` should leverage the internal job-control layer where appropriate, rather than reinventing it via `vim.uv`.

And there should be one "job id" namespace shared by `jobstart` and `vim.spawn`, so that `vim.spawn` can call `jobstop()` instead of using `uv.kill`: 

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26861
It's possible to mimic the internal job control code in user code by running the command with `detach = true` and killing it with `vim.uv.kill(-obj.pid)`. The process group is killed as expected.

Does that work on Windows?

No objection to a mitigation, but long-term we should not have 2 job-control implementations.

> Does that work on Windows?

@justinmk I'm not sure if this issue happens on Windows at all. In any case, the proc layer always detaches on unix-likes but respects `jobstart`'s `detach` option on Windows. My PR makes `vim.system` behave in the same way.

I do not think this should be treated as a `vim.system()` bug.

When I added `vim.system()`, the intent was a small wrapper around `vim.uv.spawn()` with convenient stdin/stdout/stderr handling.

In this issue, `vim.system()` starts `bash`. `bash` then creates a pipeline. Killing the process Nvim spawned and killing every descendant that process may create are different contracts. `SystemObj:kill()` currently means "signal the spawned process", not "recursively clean up a process tree".

I understand the argument that `kill()` should default to killing children too, because that is often what users mean by "stop this job". But I do not think that is a feasible default for this API. To guarantee that behavior, Nvim has to either create an ownership boundary up front, such as a Unix process group, or discover descendants later. Both choices have unavoidable tradeoffs.

Unix process semantics are not Windows process semantics. Unix has signals, process groups, sessions, and `setsid()`. Windows has different process handles and termination behavior. Making every `vim.system()` process detached/process-group-led may help with group termination on Unix, but it changes lifecycle behavior if Nvim exits or crashes. Enumerating descendants after the fact is racy and platform-specific. Mapping Unix signal behavior onto Windows is not clean either.

This is why I do not think `vim.system()` should grow job-control policy defaults. It exposes process-shaped details like `pid`, `kill(signal)`, and `code`/`signal` results. Once the API exposes those, it is already asking callers to think in terms of the underlying process model.

A real high-level job-control API would need to abstract over those details, including PIDs, process groups, signals, exit status, and what termination even means on each platform. If the API still revolves around PIDs and signal-like behavior, then it is leaking the underlying process model rather than abstracting it.

So I think the better fix is documentation, not changing the abstraction. We should be clear that `SystemObj:kill()` targets the spawned process and does not guarantee descendant cleanup. If there is a genuine portability bug in the low-level spawn behavior, that belongs in `vim.uv`/libuv or the platform layer, not in `vim.system()` growing its own partial job-control model.

@lewis6991 

> Making every `vim.system()` process detached/process-group-led may help with group termination on Unix, but it changes lifecycle behavior if Nvim exits or crashes.

In short, the "lifecyle behavior" is functionally identical, with or without `detach`.  The existing behavior is that nothing will be terminated as a result of neovim exiting/crashing.

In detail, all that POSIX specifies that is applicable here is that if a process group becomes orphaned, and contains at least one stopped process, then the entire process group is sent `SIGHUP` (to know that they've been orphaned, by default the process will terminate) followed by `SIGCONT` (so that the stopped process(es) can react to the `SIGHUP`). 

In that fringe case, the behavior will indeed differ, but in my opinion it's generally more consistent.

>Both choices have unavoidable tradeoffs.

There's no real tradeoff. I think you may have been misled by uv's documentation. The process will "will keep running after the parent process exits" no matter what because the neovim server process always creates a new session, presumably for the purpose of daemonizing. 

For it to be the case that anything at all is removed on exit, there would need to be a centralized job control layer (as proposed by @justinmk) that keeps tracks of jobs and handles terminating them (by sending `SIGHUP`) upon neovim's exit.

I should add that the most correct solution would be for neovim to only create a new process group for the spawned process, like a shell does, instead of creating a new session, but it's not possible with `uv_spawn`.

---

> I understand the argument that `kill()` should default to killing children too, because that is often what users mean by "stop this job".

It's not just what users expect, but the behavior POSIX intends as well. Job control in POSIX is defined entirely in terms of process groups, even in the case I described above the processes are handled together as a group. It is simply the idiomatic behavior on unix-likes. 

---

The intention of my PR is simply to make `vim.system` match `jobstart`'s behavior on unix-likes. They have basically the same interface and in my opinion it's clear that `vim.system` is the one misbehaving.

> When I added `vim.system()`, the intent was a small wrapper around `vim.uv.spawn()` with convenient stdin/stdout/stderr handling.
>
> 
> ... This is why I do not think `vim.system()` should grow job-control policy defaults.

Respectfully, that sounds like a cop-out. The editor exists to provide high-level interfaces that are useful and cohesive. We should not have introduced `vim.system()` if it wasn't intended to compose with "jobs", because,

1. jobs are the unit of process work that Nvim exposes,
2. they have numerous benefits
3. *not* using them leads to endless bugs that were already solved (and continue to be solved) in the internal "proc" layer (e.g. [#40074](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40074) is yet another very nasty edge case that users shouldn't have to worry about and we shouldn't have to solve twice).

## Proposal

[Here's an outline](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40267#discussion_r3443289012) of a long-term vision:

> All `vim.system` invocations will create jobs. Thus there is no need to add a `mode=job` type or special-case the impl like this.
> 
> How that is achieved, I'm not sure. It will require deep thought.
> 
> My instinct would probably be to : 
> 
> 1. start by enhancing `jobstart` however needed
> 2. let `vim.system` fully delegate to `jobstart`
> 3. in a later phase, if we don't like that because it's "yucky" ("oh noes, we're using `jobstart` instead of `vim.uv`"), then we can think about how to make it "pure lua" or whatever (perhaps by introducing `nvim_jobstart`)

> not using them leads to endless bugs that were already solved 

There is not single solution that solves all the problems. By adopting one abstraction to solve a set of edge cases, you expose bugs for another set of edge cases. I really do not think it is possible to define an API that covers all macos/linux and windows and solves every edge case, if it was libuv would have done that for us already.

If you let `vim.system` fully delegate to `jobstart` without any opt-in, it'll just break a lot of things that assume the current semantics.

> If you let `vim.system` fully delegate to `jobstart` without any opt-in, it'll just break a lot of things that assume the current semantics.

Which semantics exactly? I reviewed the above discussion; I don't see how leveraging the jobs-layer changes semantics. We can certainly have flags to control details like "detach", etc., and `vim.system` can choose defaults that maintain its current behavior.



> I really do not think it is possible to define an API that covers all macos/linux and windows and solves every edge case, if it was libuv would have done that for us already.

You can get pretty close, and that is what we've done already with `jobstart` and `:terminal`. libuv was just too lazy to do it.

> Which semantics exactly? I reviewed the above discussion; I don't see how leveraging the jobs-layer changes semantics. We can certainly have flags to control details like "detach", etc., and vim.system can choose defaults that maintain its current behavior.

The detach one is the main one I'm aware about and the one that seem to cause extra issues in windows. If we can completely preserve semantics by tailoring defaults then that sounds fine, but if semantics remain the same then I don't fully understand what problem is being solved. A lot of the edge case issues that have been quoted aren't necessarily bugs, they just appear to be results of undesirable semantics for some particular applications. In order to fix those cases, semantics need to be changed.

From what I understand the current semantics for macos/linux are fine (though some may disagree). It's only windows which is causing problems (which isn't new).

> You can get pretty close

I think it already is quite close. I'd rather keep linux semantics as it is, and patch over windows where it doesn't agree. An extra proc layer will add changes for every platform right?

Here's my take on this:

1. We shouldn't just look at `vim.system()` in isolation—the real question is whether Nvim should globally track every spawned process. Right now, a job is  a subset of a channel ID. Solving this means refactoring the architecture: creating a centralized process tracking table, and explicitly deciding if `vim.system()` spawns a tracked or not tracked,  detached process. 
#26861

2. Sorry if i am wrong i am not that knowledgeable in terms of window api. 
in Windows CLI tools like `taskkill /F /T` can cleanly nuke an entire child process tree,   if we want the job to be managed by Nvim We can use direct Win32 APIs globally either via Toolhelp32 snapshots to manually walk parent-child PIDs, or by wrapping spawned processes in native Windows Job Objects configured to kill on close when needed  bypassing standard libuv termination in somecases.

> 2\. We can use direct Win32 APIs globally either via Toolhelp32 snapshots

speak of the devil... if `vim.system()` leveraged the job system, it would get that stuff which we already implemented:  https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/08b47b196493694b48010fc5b14a2fb3be0ffdd3/src/nvim/os/proc.c#L131

milestone.description: Low priority items. We plan to work on this but don't have a target date.
milestone.title: backlog

title: job control: unify jobs layer + `vim.system` (was:  `vim.SystemObj:kill()` orphans)
